solutions.solution:
An analysis
First of all, let us see what these questions seek to test. Examining particular questions because they test your ability to derive correct inferences and your reasoning ability is thus evaluated. Also, here the examiner can check the choices clearly. Thus, there are two positive choices instead of four: "probably true" and "definitely true" and two negative choices instead of two: "probably false" and "definitely false". This requires slightly deeper thinking. Further, syllogistic reasoning has a very set pattern; there are a fixed combination of four fixed types of propositions (A, E, I, o) in inferences etc. Also, in the case of inferences, we can get very complex rules like "Some cats are not boys" or "Some cows are elephants". In this respect, passage-based inferences are different from syllogism-based inferences because in the former, the passages are from a practical world, cover everyday-life situations and are not hypothetical. Thus, it gives the examiner a wider scope to examine your Intelligence.

Having discussed the purpose of these questions (which was not of much use practically), let us come to something really important: let us examine what the difficult aspects of such questions are; or, in other words, what may make such questions difficult.

The most sensitive aspect of this question is its gradedness. Some of the inferences can be easily and quickly judged because they can be easily compared with the facts given in the passage. But, in some cases, an inference is indirect. Here, it may lead to confusion. You may vacillate between 'Definitely true' or 'probably true' or between 'probably true' and 'data inadequate' or between 'probably inadequate' and 'probably false' or between 'probably false' and 'definitely false'. For example, look at inference (3) of the same passage given above. This is straightforward and you shouldn't have any difficulty in seeing that the correct answer is 'definitely true', because the passage clearly mentions the involvement of a 'company' (Siemens) and a government agency (Department of Energy). But now, look at inferences (2) and (5). Here, you may vacillate between 'data inadequate' and 'probably true'. 'Data inadequate' will look correct because no mention has been made of what Gandhi proposed in the theory of Odgen and Williams. 'Probably true' looks correct because the passage says that the theory of Odgen and Williams began to be favourably after photovoltaic cells with higher efficiency were made. This means that there was something in the theory which got support from higher efficiency photovoltaic cells. Thus, it 'looks' true that the theory had been proposed that higher efficiency cells could be created, etc. On the other hand, in (5), you may vacillate between 'data inadequate' and 'probably false'. 'Data inadequate' may appear correct because no information is presented about the current efficiency of photovoltaic cells in America is far below 3%. In terms of area, America is not very big.
